text: The surgeon begins the procedure by reducing the hiatal hernia, visibly repositioning thirty percent of the proximal stomach back into the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon utilizes four robotic arms to apply tension and counter-traction, enhancing precision and safety during the hiatal hernia repair and Nissen fundoplication.
The surgeon enters the lesser sac at the level of the cardia, preparing the area for a tension-free Nissen fundoplication.
The surgeon advances towards the left crura, enhancing visualization of this anatomical structure during the robotic hiatal hernia repair.
The surgeon carefully preserves the peritoneum overlying the crura to optimize the repair of the hernia defect during the cruroplasty.
The surgeon focuses on the right side of the surgical field, likely addressing the anatomical structures related to the hiatal hernia repair.
The surgeon opens the gastrohepatic ligament to enhance visualization of the right crura, using robotic arms to facilitate precise manipulation.
The surgeon carefully dissects to fully mobilize and detach the hernia sac from the posterior mediastinal structures, facilitating the reduction of hernia contents.
The surgeon aims to reach the junction of the right and left crura, continuing the dissection to expose and visualize this anatomical area.
The inferior vena cava is visible to the left of the right cruz, providing anatomical context during the dissection.
The surgeon identifies the left crux and maneuvers behind the esophagus to visualize the surgical instrument at the level of the angle of His, preparing to introduce a paneral strain.
The surgeon encircles the esophagus to manipulate the gastroesophageal junction, continuing dissection within the mediastinum to free the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon continues to mobilize the structures within the mediastinum to facilitate the complete reduction of the hernia sac and its contents.
The surgeon exposes a prominent hernia sac associated with a larger hiatal hernia, visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon removes the hernia sac to minimize the chance of incorporating it during the fundoplication procedure.
The surgeon removes the hernia sac, enhancing the visualization of the gastroesophageal junction, which is crucial for the subsequent steps of the procedure.
The surgeon initiates a posterior cruroplasty using a zero-ethyl bond permanent suture, applying a figure-of-eight technique to approximate the crura.
The robotic platform is utilized, providing seven degrees of freedom for precise manipulation of surgical instruments, suture, and needle during the procedure.
The surgeon performs an anterior cruroplasty, suturing the anterior portion of the crura to reinforce the esophageal hiatus.
A fully French bougie is positioned within the esophagus and stomach for calibration, visible during the procedure.
The surgeon introduces a biological mesh to support the anterior and posterior cruroplasty, positioning it over the repaired areas for reinforcement.
The biological mesh is positioned around the esophagus, but it is visually evident that the final repair will avoid encircling the esophagus to reduce the risk of stretching.
The surgeon secures the mesh in position using interrupted sutures, ensuring it is well-placed to support the repair.
The surgeon begins the fundoplication procedure, wrapping the upper part of the stomach around the lower esophagus to reinforce the valve between the esophagus and stomach.
The surgeon places multiple gastro-gastric stitches over a distance of approximately two centimeters to create the fundoplication wrap.
The surgeon performs an earlier mobilization and treatment of the cardio, facilitating a tension-free creation of the fundoplication wrap.
The completed view shows the secured mesh in position with interrupted sutures, following the fundoplication procedure.
